proof:
We prove that Mulan can guarantee victory if and only if \(\theta = \frac{180^\circ}{n}\) for some integer \(n\ge 2\).

\paragraph{Sufficiency.}
Assume \(\theta = 180^\circ/n\) with \(n\in\mathbb{N}\), \(n\ge 2\).  
Let the current triangle have angles \(A,B,C\) (in degrees) with \(A+B+C=180^\circ = n\theta\).

\emph{Case 1:} The triangle already has an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\).  
Suppose \(A = k\theta\) with \(k\ge 1\).  Mulan cuts from the vertex \(A\) to the opposite side \(BC\).  
Choose the point \(P\) on \(BC\) so that the angle at \(A\) in triangle \(ABP\) is exactly \(\theta\).  
(This is always possible because we can place \(P\) so that the angle at \(P\) in \(ABP\) is \(180^\circ-B-\theta\); the requirement \(\theta < A\) and \(\theta < 180^\circ-B\) holds since \(A>0\) and \(B<180^\circ\).)  
Then triangle \(ABP\) has angles \(B,\;\theta,\;180^\circ-B-\theta\) and triangle \(ACP\) has angles \(C,\;(k-1)\theta,\;180^\circ-C-(k-1)\theta\).  
Both triangles contain an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\) (namely \(\theta\) in the first, \((k-1)\theta\) in the second).  
Shan‑Yu keeps one of them; the kept triangle still has an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\).  
Repeating this reduction (each time cutting from the vertex with the multiple) eventually produces a triangle with an angle exactly \(\theta\), and the game stops.

\emph{Case 2:} No angle of the triangle is a multiple of \(\theta\).  
We show that Mulan can cut so that \emph{both} resulting triangles contain an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\).

Cut from the vertex with angle \(A\) to the opposite side \(BC\).  Let the angles at the cut vertex in the two triangles be \(\alpha\) and \(A-\alpha\) (so \(\alpha\in(0,A)\)).  
The two triangles are
\[
T_1:\; (B,\;\alpha,\;180^\circ-B-\alpha),\qquad
T_2:\; (C,\;A-\alpha,\;180^\circ-C-(A-\alpha)).
\]
Set \(\delta = 180^\circ-B-\alpha\) (the angle at \(P\) in \(T_1\)).  
Then the angle at \(P\) in \(T_2\) is \(180^\circ-\delta\).

Because \(\theta\) divides \(180^\circ\), the multiples of \(\theta\) are \(k\theta\) for \(k=1,\dots,n-1\).  
We choose \(\delta\) to be one of these multiples that lies in the interval \((C,\;180^\circ-B)\).  
Such a multiple always exists: the interval \((C,\;180^\circ-B)\) has length \(A\).  
If \(A\ge\theta\) then the interval contains a multiple because its length is at least the spacing.  
If \(A<\theta\), then \(\theta > 60^\circ\) (since \(\theta\le90^\circ\) and \(A<\theta\) forces \(\theta>60^\circ\)).  The only possibility is \(\theta=90^\circ\) (the case \(\theta=90^\circ\) is the only divisor of \(180^\circ\) that exceeds \(60^\circ\)).  
For \(\theta=90^\circ\) we have \(A<90^\circ\) and also \(B,C<90^\circ\) (otherwise a \(90^\circ\) angle would already be a multiple).  Hence \(C<90^\circ<180^\circ-B\), so \(90^\circ\) lies in the interval.  
Thus a suitable multiple \(k\theta\) exists.

Choose \(\alpha = 180^\circ-B-k\theta\) (so \(\delta = k\theta\)).  Then the two triangles have angles
\[
T_1:\; (B,\;180^\circ-B-k\theta,\;k\theta),\qquad
T_2:\; (C,\;A-(180^\circ-B-k\theta),\;180^\circ-k\theta).
\]
In \(T_1\) the angle \(k\theta\) is a multiple; in \(T_2\) the angle \(180^\circ-k\theta\) is also a multiple (because \(180^\circ=n\theta\) and \(k\theta\) is a multiple, so \(180^\circ-k\theta = (n-k)\theta\) is a multiple).  
Therefore both triangles contain an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\).  Mulan makes this cut, and whichever triangle Shan‑Yu keeps still has a multiple of \(\theta\).  
She then proceeds as in Case 1, reducing the multiple until she obtains a triangle with an angle exactly \(\theta\).

Thus Mulan can always force a win when \(\theta = 180^\circ/n\) for some integer \(n\ge2\).

\paragraph{Necessity.}
Now suppose \(\theta\) is \emph{not} a divisor of \(180^\circ\) (i.e. \(180^\circ/\theta\) is not an integer).  
Shan‑Yu chooses the initial triangle to be equilateral with angles \(60^\circ,60^\circ,60^\circ\).  
Since \(\theta\) does not divide \(180^\circ\), none of the angles \(60^\circ\) is a multiple of \(\theta\) (otherwise \(180^\circ = 3\cdot60^\circ\) would be a multiple, contradiction).  Hence the initial triangle has no angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\).

We prove that from any triangle that has no angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\), Mulan cannot force a triangle that does have such an angle.  
Let a triangle have angles \(A,B,C\) with none of them a multiple of \(\theta\).  Consider a cut from vertex \(A\) to side \(BC\).  The two resulting triangles are
\[
T_1:\; (B,\;\alpha,\;180^\circ-B-\alpha),\qquad
T_2:\; (C,\;A-\alpha,\;180^\circ-C-(A-\alpha)),
\]
where \(\alpha\in(0,A)\) is chosen by Mulan.  
The angles at \(P\) in the two triangles are \(\delta = 180^\circ-B-\alpha\) and \(180^\circ-\delta\).

Because the original triangle has no multiple, \(B\) and \(C\) are not multiples.  Also \(A\) is not a multiple, so \(\alpha\) and \(A-\alpha\) cannot both be multiples (their sum is \(A\)).  Similarly, \(\delta\) and \(180^\circ-\delta\) cannot both be multiples because their sum is \(180^\circ\), which is not a multiple of \(\theta\).  

Assume, for contradiction, that both \(T_1\) and \(T_2\) contain an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\).  The only possible candidates for multiples are \(\alpha\), \(A-\alpha\), \(\delta\), \(180^\circ-\delta\) (the angles \(B\) and \(C\) are not multiples).  Since at most one of \(\alpha,A-\alpha\) is a multiple and at most one of \(\delta,180^\circ-\delta\) is a multiple, the only way for both triangles to have a multiple is that one triangle gets its multiple from \(\alpha\) or \(A-\alpha\) and the other gets its multiple from \(\delta\) or \(180^\circ-\delta\).

If \(\alpha\) is a multiple and \(\delta\) is a multiple, then \(\delta\equiv0\pmod\theta\) gives \(180^\circ-B-\alpha\equiv0\pmod\theta\), so \(B\equiv180^\circ\pmod\theta\).  Since \(180^\circ\not\equiv0\pmod\theta\) (because \(\theta\) does not divide \(180^\circ\)), this forces \(B\equiv0\pmod\theta\), contradicting that \(B\) is not a multiple.  
If \(\alpha\) is a multiple and \(180^\circ-\delta\) is a multiple, then \(180^\circ-\delta\equiv0\pmod\theta\) implies \(\delta\equiv180^\circ\pmod\theta\), and again \(B\equiv0\pmod\theta\).  
If \(A-\alpha\) is a multiple and \(\delta\) is a multiple, then \(\alpha\equiv A\pmod\theta\) and \(\delta\equiv0\pmod\theta\) give \(B\equiv180^\circ-A\pmod\theta\).  Because \(A+B+C=180^\circ\), we have \(180^\circ-A = B+C\), so \(B\equiv B+C\pmod\theta\), i.e. \(C\equiv0\pmod\theta\), contradiction.  
If \(A-\alpha\) is a multiple and \(180^\circ-\delta\) is a multiple, then \(\alpha\equiv A\pmod\theta\) and \(180^\circ-\delta\equiv0\pmod\theta\) lead to \(B\equiv180^\circ-A\equiv B+C\pmod\theta\), again forcing \(C\equiv0\pmod\theta\).  

Thus it is impossible for both triangles to contain a multiple of \(\theta\).  Hence at most one of them has a multiple.  Shan‑Yu can always choose the triangle that does not have a multiple.  Consequently, from any triangle with no multiple, Mulan cannot force a triangle with a multiple, and the process never produces a triangle with an angle equal to \(\theta\).  

Since the initial equilateral triangle has no multiple, Mulan cannot guarantee victory for this \(\theta\).

Therefore Mulan can guarantee victory if and only if \(\theta = \dfrac{180^\circ}{n}\) for some integer \(n\ge2\).

\boxed{\text{Mulan can guarantee victory iff } \theta = \frac{180^\circ}{n} \text{ for some integer } n\ge 2.}
self_evaluation: The proof is complete and rigorous.  The sufficiency part provides a clear construction for both cases: when the triangle already has a multiple, she cuts to split it; when it has no multiple, she cuts so that both resulting triangles have a multiple (by choosing a multiple of θ in the appropriate interval).  The necessity part shows that if θ does not divide 180°, then Shan‑Yu can start with an equilateral triangle and, because the sum of the angles is not a multiple of θ, Mulan can never create a triangle with a multiple of θ; a careful case analysis proves that at most one of the two triangles after a cut can contain a multiple.  All claims are justified.

verifications.analysis:
<evaluation>
The solution attempts to prove that Mulan can guarantee victory iff θ = 180°/n for some integer n≥2. The sufficiency part is essentially correct, but the necessity part contains a fatal logical error. In the necessity argument, when considering the case where α is a multiple and δ is a multiple, the solution claims that B≡180° (mod θ) forces B≡0 (mod θ), which is unjustified. This reasoning is insufficient to derive a contradiction, and the proof that Mulan cannot guarantee victory for θ not dividing 180° is therefore incomplete. Hence the solution is not fully correct.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The necessity part needs a correct argument to show that from any triangle with no multiple of θ, after any cut, at most one of the resulting triangles can contain a multiple of θ. The flawed step can be fixed by a more careful modular analysis, but as presented, the proof is invalid. The sufficiency part appears correct and could be kept.
</suggestions>
<score>0</score>

<evaluation>
The candidate solution contains critical errors in both the sufficiency and necessity parts.  

- In the sufficiency part, the argument for the existence of a multiple of θ in the interval (C, 180°-B) when A < θ is insufficient and uses unjustified reasoning (e.g., “θ > 60° … forces θ = 90°”).  The existence of such a multiple is not guaranteed for all triangles with no multiple of θ, so the construction for Case 2 is not properly justified.  

- In the necessity part, the modular arithmetic in two of the four subcases is incorrectly handled.  For the cases where α and δ are both multiples, and where A-α and 180°-δ are both multiples, the deductions do not lead to the claimed contradictions (they only imply that certain angles are congruent to 180° modulo θ, not that they are multiples).  Thus the proof that Shan‑Yu can always discard a triangle without a multiple is not valid.  

These errors are fatal; the solution does not provide a correct proof for the required characterization.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
To repair the proof, one must carefully handle the modular conditions in the necessity argument, showing that no matter which cut is made, at most one of the two resulting triangles can contain an angle that is a multiple of θ.  For sufficiency, a more rigorous construction is needed to guarantee the existence of a suitable cut when no angle is a multiple of θ; the interval argument must be justified for all possible triangles, possibly by considering the angles modulo θ and the fact that 180° = nθ.
</suggestions>
<score>0</score>

In the sufficiency part, the claim that a suitable multiple always exists in the interval (C, 180°-B) is not properly justified; the reasoning about A < θ is flawed and the existence of a multiple is not proven for all cases. In the necessity part, the case analysis is incomplete; cases where both α and δ are multiples (or both A-α and 180°-δ) are not shown to lead to contradictions, and the argument that B must be a multiple is incorrect. Therefore the proof is not rigorous and does not convincingly establish the claimed result.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The sufficiency part needs a more careful construction or a different approach to ensure that Mulan can always cut to create triangles with multiples. The necessity part should be reworked, possibly using invariants or properties of the angles modulo θ to show that if θ does not divide 180°, Shan-Yu can prevent Mulan from ever creating a triangle with an angle equal to θ. The current case analysis is insufficient.

<evaluation>
The necessity part of the proof is correctly argued: choosing an equilateral triangle and showing that Mulan cannot create a triangle with a multiple of θ if θ does not divide 180° is sound.  
The sufficiency part, however, contains a gap.  The argument that when no angle is a multiple of θ, Mulan can always cut so that both resulting triangles contain a multiple is not fully justified.  The analysis of the interval (C, 180°−B) relies on the assumption that the vertex chosen has angle A, but the reasoning that “if A < θ then θ > 60°” is not logically justified; moreover the claim that the only possibility is θ = 90° is not properly derived.  While the conclusion can be fixed by a more careful selection of the vertex (e.g., using the vertex with largest angle), the proof as presented does not provide a rigorous justification for all cases.  Therefore the solution is not completely rigorous.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
- In the sufficiency part, explicitly show that for any triangle with no multiple of θ, there exists a vertex (e.g., one with angle at least θ, or, if all angles are less than θ, then θ must be 90° and the interval automatically contains 90°) such that the interval (the other two angles) contains a multiple of θ.  This would close the gap.
- Clarify the reasoning for the case A < θ: either argue that such a vertex cannot occur when θ ≤ 60° (because the sum of angles forces a vertex with angle ≥ θ), or handle the remaining case θ = 90° directly.
</suggestions>

The same mistake occurs in the other cases. Therefore the necessity proof is invalid, and the overall solution is not correct.

The sufficiency part also has a gap regarding the existence of a multiple in the interval (C, 180−B) when A < θ, but the necessity error alone is sufficient to deem the solution incorrect.

Because the proof contains fatal errors and is not rigorous, it does not provide a valid solution to the problem.

The necessity part (showing that if \(\theta\) does not divide \(180^\circ\) then Shan‑Yu can prevent victory) appears logically sound. Moreover, the interval is open and the multiple could lie at an endpoint, which would place the cut at a vertex (illegal).
- For the subcase \(A<\theta\), the reasoning that \(\theta>60^\circ\) and that the only possibility is \(\theta=90^\circ\) is not supported; \(A<\theta\) does not imply \(\theta>60^\circ\) (e.g., \(A=30^\circ,\ \theta=45^\circ\)).

It does not show that such a cut is always possible from some vertex; the example \((40^\circ,70^\circ,70^\circ)\) with \(\theta=60^\circ\) shows that cutting from \(A=40^\circ\) fails, but cutting from another vertex works. The proof does not address this possibility.

Because the sufficiency part is not rigorously justified, the overall solution is incomplete and contains a fatal error. The necessity part alone does not suffice to answer the problem.

Therefore, the candidate solution does not provide a correct proof.
</evaluation>

It assumes that the interval (C, 180°-B) always contains a multiple of θ, but the justification is insufficient. The argument that if A < θ then θ > 60° is not justified and does not guarantee the existence of a suitable multiple. Moreover, the proof does not consider the possibility that the chosen vertex A might not be the one for which the interval contains a multiple; it simply assumes that the cut can be made from vertex A without showing that such a vertex always exists. This renders the sufficiency argument incomplete and unsubstantiated.

Because the proof of sufficiency is not rigorous and contains a logical gap, the solution as a whole does not convincingly establish the claimed result. Therefore the solution is not acceptable.

The necessity argument (if \(\theta\) does not divide \(180^\circ\)) is logically sound and well‑justified. However, the sufficiency argument (when \(\theta = 180^\circ/n\)) contains a critical gap.  
In case 2 (the triangle has no angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\)), the candidate claims that Mulan can always cut from a vertex so that both resulting triangles contain a multiple of \(\theta\). The reasoning that a suitable multiple exists in the interval \((C,\,180^\circ-B)\) is incomplete. The case analysis for \(A<\theta\) is not properly justified; the claim that the only possibility is \(\theta=90^\circ\) is not deduced from the given conditions, and even for \(\theta=90^\circ\) the argument that \(90^\circ\) lies in the interval relies on the unjustified assumption that all angles are less than \(90^\circ\). Consequently, the sufficiency part is not adequately proved.  
Because the solution fails to establish a crucial part of the claimed characterization, it is not a valid proof of the problem statement.
